messages.author: Seacourt
messages.text: Hey all: newby here (but experienced software engineer). Has anyone been working on building a map that has not just the repeaters, but the nodes that each repeater can reliably see zero-hop? I guess this would require some mechanism to upload that information from each repeater regularly to the internet. But feels like it would be super useful to start working out how fully connected the network is in a region, and if you're building out nodes - what directions you should be adding in to connect isolated subgraphs etc.
messages.content_html: Hey all: newby here (but experienced software engineer). Has anyone been working on building a map that has not just the repeaters, but the nodes that each repeater can reliably see zero-hop? I guess this would require some mechanism to upload that information from each repeater regularly to the internet. But feels like it would be super useful to start working out how fully connected the network is in a region, and if you're building out nodes - what directions you should be adding in to connect isolated subgraphs etc.

messages.author: Seacourt
messages.text: It doesn't look like it currently has a way to visualise or export the connectivity graph though?
messages.content_html: It doesn't look like it currently has a way to visualise or export the connectivity graph though?

messages.author: Seacourt
messages.content_html: Oh. That's very cool.

messages.author: Seacourt
messages.text: Ok. That makes sense. I'm coming at this from being in a coverage desert, and wondering how best to spend resources (likely multi hop) trying to connect to a well-connected bit of the existing network. But it's knowing which bit is well connected...
messages.content_html: Ok. That makes sense. I'm coming at this from being in a coverage desert, and wondering how best to spend resources (likely multi hop) trying to connect to a well-connected bit of the existing network. But it's knowing which bit is well connected...

messages.author: Luc [Chingford Omni]
messages.text: I would suggest the trick there is to look at messages in the public channel on letsmesh and then see what paths they were routed over, to see repeaters near you that are getting in and out to other neighbours
messages.content_html: I would suggest the trick there is to look at messages in the public channel on letsmesh and then see what paths they were routed over, to see repeaters near you that are getting in and out to other neighbours
messages.reply_html:
Seacourt
Ok. That makes sense. I'm coming at this from being in a coverage desert, and wondering how best to spend resources (likely multi hop) trying to connect to a well-connected bit of the existing network. But it's knowing which bit is well connected...
